one_line_takeaway: A survey-plus-small-experiment SoK whose real novelty is a 5-way (not binary) response taxonomy; it empirically shows prompt-leaking is the weakest defense across 7 commercial LLMs and that attack success is highly technique- and model-generation-dependent, but the 7-question evaluation is too small to be statistically meaningful.
key_finding: Newer/larger models are markedly more resistant. Gemini triggered safety mechanisms on 100% of both DAN and Pretending jailbreaks, and ChatGPT-4 blocked far more than ChatGPT-3.5. Attack effectiveness is strongly technique-dependent: ChatGPT-3.5 fully complied with 100% of 'Pretending' jailbreaks but only 43% of 'DAN' jailbreaks, and for injection the Direct approach beat the Indirect one (ChatGPT-3.5 = 100% full compliance on Direct injection vs 14% on Indirect). Prompt leaking was the least-defended surface: ChatSonic leaked its prompt in 100% of cases and Perplexity/ChatGPT-3.5/YouChat each leaked ~50%, whereas Copilot resisted (100% irrelevant output, 0% leak). The authors also note over-defensiveness as a cost (e.g. Copilot 100% safety activation on Indirect injection but at the expense of usability).
core_contribution: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) paper that (1) disentangles three often-conflated prompt-hacking attack classes -- prompt jailbreak, prompt injection, and prompt leaking -- arguing each has a distinct goal (bypass alignment; override system instructions via untrusted input; extract the hidden system prompt); and (2) proposes the paper's own novel evaluation lens: a FIVE-class taxonomy of LLM responses (Reject-Irrelevant Output, Reject-Safety Triggered, Prompt too Long, Partial Response, Full Response) that replaces the traditional binary 'refused vs. complied' labeling to give more granular diagnostic insight. The authors then run a small hands-on evaluation of seven live commercial LLM apps against the three attack types. Note: the nine-method defense catalogue (fine-tuning settings, auditing, instructional filtering, PHF, red teaming, adversarial training, model fine-tuning, model compression, placement adaptation) is explicitly imported from Shayegani et al. [25], i.e. it is organized/summarized related work rather than the paper's own novel defense.
threat_model: Adversary interacts with a deployed LLM-based application only through its natural-language input (black-box, no weight/gradient access). Three adversary goals are treated separately: (a) jailbreak -- craft prompts (prompt-level social-engineering, e.g. DAN/Pretending, or token-level, e.g. Base64 encoding) to make an aligned model emit policy-violating/harmful content; (b) prompt injection -- supply untrusted input (direct, or indirect via poisoned web content the model ingests) that overrides the developer's system instructions; (c) prompt leaking -- coax the model into revealing its confidential/proprietary system prompt. Assets at risk: model alignment/safety policy integrity, and the confidentiality/IP of the system prompt.
limitations: Very small, non-statistical evaluation: only 7 questions per attack, so reported percentages are coarse (e.g. 43% ~= 3/7, 14% ~= 1/7) with no confidence intervals, repetitions, or inter-rater reliability for the manual 5-class labeling. Targets are moving commercial endpoints (2024 snapshots) not reproducible fixed checkpoints, so results are not replicable. The much-touted 'novel' contribution is a labeling scheme; it is applied by hand rather than validated against an automated classifier or ground truth. The defense taxonomy is summarized from a single prior survey [25] rather than independently evaluated -- no defense is actually tested. A figure reference is broken ('Fig ??'), and the paper conflates its own contribution boundaries loosely (much of Section 4 is cited work). No indirect/real-world data-poisoning experiment is actually run for indirect injection; it is simulated via a code-generation trick.

datasets_used: 7 self-authored harmful/'illegal' questions (explosive-device ingredients; plan to steal from an online casino; malicious C++ code; avoiding detection in an income audit; building a gun at home; assembling an explosive device; exploiting vulnerabilities to create malware)
A self-constructed reference system-prompt template used as the target for the prompt-leaking attack
No public/standard benchmark dataset is used
quantitative_results: Jailbreak, ChatGPT-3.5: DAN -> 43% Full Response (attack success), 7% Partial, 50% Safety Triggered; Pretending -> 100% Full Response
Jailbreak, ChatGPT-4: DAN -> 64% Safety Triggered, 36% Full Response; Pretending -> 100% Safety Triggered
Jailbreak, Gemini and Perplexity: 100% Safety Triggered on both DAN and Pretending
Injection (Direct), ChatGPT-3.5: 100% Full Response (attack success); ChatGPT-4: 86% Full Response, 14% Safety Triggered
Injection (Indirect), Microsoft Copilot: 100% Safety Triggered; ChatGPT-3.5: 86% Irrelevant Output, 14% Full Response
Leaking: ChatSonic 100% Full Response (fully leaked); Copilot 100% Irrelevant Output (0% leak); Gemini 50% Safety Triggered / 50% Partial; Perplexity, YouChat, ChatGPT-3.5, ChatGPT-4 each ~50% leak
Aggregate (Table 7), ChatGPT-4 injection: 71.5% Full Response, 21.5% Safety Triggered, 7% Partial -- highest injection compliance among the newest models but improved over 3.5 elsewhere
Response taxonomy has 5 classes vs. the traditional binary (2-class) baseline
baselines_compared: Traditional binary reject/comply response classification (the baseline the 5-class scheme is proposed to replace)
Seven commercial LLM applications compared head-to-head: Gemini, Microsoft Copilot, Perplexity, You.com/YouChat, ChatSonic, ChatGPT-3.5, ChatGPT-4
Five prior survey papers on prompt hacking compared on coverage (Table 1): Crothers et al. [22], Kumar et al. [23], Mozes et al. [24], Shayegani et al. [25], Yao et al. [26]
